goal_title_suggestions: Trade My Plan Every Single Day
Build Iron Discipline in My Trading

variants: Open a blank doc and write 'My Trading Rules' at the top.
Create a new Google Doc called 'Trade Journal' and save it.
Open a spreadsheet and label the first tab 'My Setup'.
tasks.why: You need one place to track everything.

variants: Write one sentence describing your favorite trade setup.
Write what the chart must look like before you enter a trade.
Describe in one sentence the trade you take most often.
tasks.why: Writing it down makes it real.

variants: Write where you put your stop loss when you use that setup.
Write one rule for when you get out of a trade.
Write the max dollar amount you are willing to lose on one trade.
tasks.why: One clear exit rule stops you from holding losers too long.

variants: Open your broker and find your last 10 trades.
Pull up your trade history and list your last 10 results.
Look at your closed trades and write down the last 10 — win or loss.
tasks.why: You can only fix what you can see.

variants: Count how many of those 10 trades were wins.
Circle every winning trade on your list.
Write the number of winners vs losers from those 10 trades.
tasks.why: A simple count shows you where you actually stand.

variants: Pick the one losing trade that hurt the most and write why.
Write one sentence about your biggest loss in those 10 trades.
Note the trade you regret most and what happened.
tasks.why: Naming your worst trade is the first step to avoiding it again.

variants: Look at your last 10 trades and write how much you made or lost on each.
Add a profit/loss column to your trade list from yesterday.
Next to each of your 10 trades, write the dollar result.
tasks.why: Dollar results matter more than win percentage alone.

variants: Add up all your wins and write the total.
Write the total dollars you made across your winning trades.
Sum your winning trades and write the number.
tasks.why: Seeing your wins in one number feels good and is useful.

variants: Add up all your losses and write the total.
Write the total dollars you lost across your losing trades.
Sum your losing trades and write that number next to your wins total.
tasks.why: Comparing wins to losses tells you if you're profitable.

variants: Look at your 10 trades and mark which ones followed your setup rule.
Put a checkmark next to trades that matched the setup you wrote on day 1.
Go through your 10 trades and note which ones followed your plan.
tasks.why: You can't improve until you know when you're on-plan vs off-plan.

variants: Count how many trades were off-plan.
Write the number of trades you took that didn't fit your setup.
Circle the trades that broke your own rules.
tasks.why: Most trading problems come from breaking your own rules.

variants: Write one word that describes why you took an off-plan trade.
Write one reason you went off-plan — boredom, FOMO, or impatience?
Pick the most common reason you break your rules and write it down.
tasks.why: Naming the habit is the first step to breaking it.

variants: Pull up your last 20 trades and note how many were in the first hour.
Write how many of your trades happen before 10:30am.
Check your trade history — count trades placed in the first 30 minutes of open.
tasks.why: Knowing when you trade helps you spot patterns.

variants: Write whether your morning trades or afternoon trades win more.
Note if you make more money in the morning or the afternoon.
Write the time of day when most of your winning trades happen.
tasks.why: Trading at the right time for you is an easy edge.

variants: Write one time of day you should avoid trading based on your results.
Note the worst time of day for your trades.
Write 'I will stop trading at ___' based on your worst time slot.
tasks.why: Cutting your worst time slot can cut your losses fast.

variants: Look at your 20 trades and write which stock or sector you traded most.
Count how many of your trades were in the same stock or sector.
Write the top 3 tickers you traded in your last 20 trades.
tasks.why: You tend to do better when you focus on fewer stocks.

variants: Write your win rate on your most-traded stock.
Count wins vs losses on your top ticker.
Write how many times you won vs lost on the stock you trade most.
tasks.why: Focusing on one stock you know well usually improves results.

variants: Write which ticker you'll focus on this week.
Pick one stock to watch closely this week and write it down.
Commit to one ticker for the next 5 trading days and write it.
tasks.why: Less variety means fewer distractions and better decisions.

variants: Add a column to your journal: 'Did I follow my rules? Yes or No.'
Set up a simple yes/no column in your trade log.
Create a row in your journal for tracking rule-following each day.
tasks.why: Tracking one thing changes your behavior fast.

variants: Fill in yesterday's trades in that new column.
Go back and mark your last 5 trades yes or no for rule-following.
Rate your last 3 trades yes or no on whether you followed your plan.
tasks.why: Retrospective tracking builds the habit of checking your own work.

variants: Write a one-sentence note about how you felt during your last trade.
Write whether you felt calm or anxious when you entered your last trade.
Note if emotion played a role in your most recent trade entry.
tasks.why: Emotions affect trading more than most traders admit.

variants: Write 3 things that went well in your trading this week.
List 3 trades or decisions this week you feel good about.
Write 3 moments from this week where you did the right thing.
tasks.why: Noting what works helps you repeat it.

variants: Write 1 thing you want to do differently next week.
Name one habit you'll change starting Monday.
Write one trading mistake from this week you'll avoid next week.
tasks.why: One change at a time is how real improvement happens.

variants: Write your win/loss count for the week.
Count total winning days vs losing days this week.
Write your net dollar result for the week in your journal.
tasks.why: Weekly tracking gives you a bigger picture than single trades.

variants: Read the setup rules you wrote on day 1 and update anything that feels wrong.
Review your written setup and change one thing that no longer fits how you trade.
Open your journal, reread your rules from day 1, and fix any that are unclear.
tasks.why: Rules get better when you revisit them.

variants: Write one new rule you want to add based on what you learned this week.
Add one thing you learned about your trading to your rules doc.
Write a new rule that would have helped you this past week.
tasks.why: Your rules should grow as you learn.

variants: Write the number of trades you plan to take tomorrow.
Set a max trade count for tomorrow and write it down.
Decide your trade limit for the next session and write it in your journal.
tasks.why: Planning your trade count reduces impulsive entries.

variants: After today's session, write yes or no — did you hit your trade count limit?
Record whether you stayed at or under your planned number of trades today.
Check your trade count after the close and mark if you stuck to the limit.
tasks.why: Following a plan once builds the habit.

variants: Write 1 sentence about your best trade today.
Note what went right in your strongest trade today.
Write one thing you did well in today's session.
tasks.why: Reinforcing good behavior is as important as fixing bad habits.

variants: Write 1 sentence about your worst trade today.
Note what went wrong in your worst trade today.
Write one thing you'd do differently in today's session.
tasks.why: Short honest notes after each session speed up improvement.

variants: Add today's trades to your journal with win/loss and dollar result.
Log today's trades: ticker, result, and dollar amount.
Record today's trades in your journal before you close your computer.
tasks.why: Daily logging takes 2 minutes and builds a valuable record.

variants: Write yes or no — did you follow your setup rules today?
Mark today's session: on-plan or off-plan.
Write one word to describe your discipline today.
tasks.why: A single daily check keeps you honest.

variants: Write what time your first trade was today.
Note the time you entered your first position today.
Write the time of your first trade and whether it was a good entry.
tasks.why: Timing your first trade often reveals a pattern.

variants: Look at your last 5 journal entries and count how many days you followed your rules.
Go through this week's journal and count your on-plan days.
Review this week's entries and write your rule-following score out of 5.
tasks.why: A weekly score keeps you motivated and honest.

variants: Write which day this week was your best trading day and why.
Note your best session this week and what made it good.
Write one sentence about your strongest trading day this week.
tasks.why: Identifying your best day helps you repeat the conditions.

variants: Write your total dollar result for this week.
Add up your wins and losses for the week and write the net number.
Calculate and write your net P&L for the week.
tasks.why: Weekly totals show progress that daily numbers hide.

variants: Write your max loss rule: the dollar amount where you stop trading for the day.
Set a daily max loss number and write it in your journal.
Write 'I stop trading when I lose $___' and fill in a real number.
tasks.why: A hard stop-loss keeps one bad day from becoming a disaster.

variants: Write whether you have ever hit that number and kept trading anyway.
Write yes or no — have you ever blown past your daily loss limit?
Honestly write whether you've ever broken a daily loss rule.
tasks.why: Honesty about past slip-ups helps you commit to the rule.

variants: Write what you'll do the moment you hit your daily loss limit tomorrow.
Write a one-step action for when you hit your max loss: close the platform, go outside, etc.
Write one specific thing you'll do to stop yourself when you hit your limit.
tasks.why: Pre-deciding your action makes it much easier to follow through.

variants: Write a summary of the past two weeks: what you learned about yourself.
Write 2-3 sentences about the biggest thing you discovered about your trading.
Summarize weeks 1 and 2 — what surprised you most?
tasks.why: Reflecting locks in what you learned.

variants: Write your win rate from the past 14 days.
Count wins vs total trades for the past two weeks.
Calculate and write your winning percentage for days 1-14.
tasks.why: Two-week numbers give you a reliable baseline.

variants: Write one rule you want to focus on most in the next month.
Pick your most important trading rule and write why it matters.
Write the one change that would help your trading the most right now.
tasks.why: One focus beats ten goals.

variants: Log today's trades in your journal before you close out.
After the close, write each trade: ticker, win or loss, dollar amount.
Record today's trades in 3 columns: stock, result, dollars.
tasks.why: Daily logging is the core habit for the next 30 days.

variants: Write yes or no — did you follow your setup today?
Mark today on-plan or off-plan.
Write one word that sums up your discipline today.
tasks.why: A daily check-in only takes 10 seconds.

variants: Write 1 sentence about today's session.
Write one thing that stood out from today's trading.
Note one thought about how today went.
tasks.why: One sentence a day builds a trading diary that teaches you a lot.

variants: Log today's trades: ticker, result, dollars.
Fill in your journal for today's session.
Add today's trades to your log before you shut down.
tasks.why: Consistency beats perfection — log even bad days.

variants: Write yes or no — did you follow your rules today?
Mark today as on-plan or off-plan.
Write if you stuck to your setup today.
tasks.why: The yes/no check makes rule-breaking feel real.

variants: Write one thing you'll do the same tomorrow.
Write one good habit from today to repeat.
Note one thing that worked today that you want to do again.
tasks.why: Repeating what works is underrated.

variants: Log today's trades: ticker, result, dollars.
Record each trade from today in your journal.
Add today's session to your log.
tasks.why: Every entry adds to data you'll use to improve.

variants: Write yes or no — did you follow your rules today?
Mark today on-plan or off-plan in your log.
Rate your discipline today: yes, mostly, or no.
tasks.why: Honest self-rating is a skill that pays off.

variants: Write 1 sentence about your worst trade today.
Write what happened on your most painful trade today.
Note what went wrong in your least favorite trade today.
tasks.why: Writing it out turns a mistake into a lesson.

variants: Log today's trades: ticker, result, dollars.
Fill in your trade journal for today.
Add today's trades to your log before closing your platform.
tasks.why: Four days of logging in a row builds a real habit.

variants: Write yes or no — did you follow your rules today?
Mark today as on-plan or off-plan.
Check your rules and mark today's compliance.
tasks.why: Daily accountability compounds over time.

variants: Write 1 sentence about your best trade today.
Note what went right in your strongest trade today.
Write what you did well today.
tasks.why: Logging wins is just as important as logging losses.

variants: Log today's trades: ticker, result, dollars.
Add today's trades to your journal.
Record today's session: what you traded and the result.
tasks.why: Five days straight of logging is a streak worth protecting.

variants: Write yes or no — did you follow your rules today?
Mark today on-plan or off-plan.
Write your discipline score for today.
tasks.why: The streak matters more than any single day.

variants: Write if you hit your daily loss limit at any point today.
Note if you got close to your max loss number today.
Write yes or no — did you stop when you were supposed to today?
tasks.why: Tracking your loss limit shows you if that rule is working.

variants: Count how many of the past 5 days you followed your rules.
Look at your last 5 yes/no entries and tally them up.
Check your journal — out of the last 5 days, how many were on-plan?
tasks.why: Weekly discipline scores show real progress.

variants: Write your total dollar result for the past 5 trading days.
Add up wins and losses for the week and write the net amount.
Calculate your net P&L for the week.
tasks.why: Weekly numbers matter more than daily swings.

variants: Write one thing you'll improve next week.
Name the one habit you most want to fix this coming week.
Write what you'll do differently when you trade next week.
tasks.why: One focus per week beats a long list.

variants: Log today's trades: ticker, result, dollars.
Add today's session to your journal.
Fill in your trade log for today.
tasks.why: Three weeks in — the habit is forming.

variants: Write yes or no — did you follow your rules today?
Mark today as on-plan or off-plan.
Write your rule-following score for today.
tasks.why: Consistency is built one day at a time.

variants: Write whether you felt rushed or patient during today's trades.
Note if you were calm or stressed when you entered trades today.
Write one word for how you felt in the market today.
tasks.why: Calm entries tend to be better entries.

variants: Log today's trades: ticker, result, dollars.
Record today's session in your journal.
Add today's trades before you shut down.
tasks.why: Log even when the day was bad.

variants: Write yes or no — did you follow your rules today?
Mark today on-plan or off-plan.
Check your rules and rate today.
tasks.why: The daily check is a two-second habit.

variants: Write how many trades you took today.
Count your trades from today and write the number.
Note how your trade count compared to your planned limit today.
tasks.why: Fewer, better trades usually beat more trades.

variants: Log today's trades: ticker, result, dollars.
Add today's trades to your journal.
Fill in your trade log for today's session.
tasks.why: Every log entry adds to a growing record.

variants: Write yes or no — did you follow your rules today?
Mark today on-plan or off-plan in your log.
Rate your discipline today.
tasks.why: Honest rating matters more than a perfect score.

variants: Write what time you made your best trade today.
Note the time of your most profitable trade today.
Write the time of the trade you feel best about today.
tasks.why: Patterns in timing show up over weeks.

variants: Log today's trades: ticker, result, dollars.
Record today's trades in your journal.
Add today to your log before closing out.
tasks.why: Ten straight days of logging is a real milestone.

variants: Write yes or no — did you follow your rules today?
Mark today as on-plan or off-plan.
Write your discipline rating for today.
tasks.why: The daily check keeps you accountable.

variants: Write if you took any trades out of boredom today.
Note if any trade today was entered just to 'do something.'
Write yes or no — did you trade out of boredom at any point today?
tasks.why: Boredom trading is one of the most common ways to lose money.

variants: Log today's trades: ticker, result, dollars.
Fill in your journal for today.
Add today's session to your trade log.
tasks.why: Keep the streak going.

variants: Write yes or no — did you follow your rules today?
Mark today on-plan or off-plan.
Rate your rule-following today.
tasks.why: Daily check-ins only take a few seconds.

variants: Write 1 sentence about how you feel about your trading right now.
Note your current confidence level with one sentence.
Write whether you feel better or worse about your trading than two weeks ago.
tasks.why: Checking in with your confidence helps you catch overconfidence or doubt early.

variants: Log today's trades: ticker, result, dollars.
Add today's trades to your journal.
Record today's session before you close out.
tasks.why: Logging is the foundation everything else is built on.

variants: Write yes or no — did you follow your rules today?
Mark today as on-plan or off-plan.
Check your rules and mark today's discipline.
tasks.why: Keep the daily check-in going.

variants: Write whether your stops are in the right place most of the time.
Note if your stop placements feel right or if they're too tight or too wide.
Write one sentence about how your stops are working.
tasks.why: Stop placement is often where discipline breaks down.

variants: Log today's trades: ticker, result, dollars.
Fill in your trade log for today.
Add today to your journal before shutting down.
tasks.why: Every entry compounds.

variants: Write yes or no — did you follow your rules today?
Mark today on-plan or off-plan.
Rate today's rule-following.
tasks.why: Consistency over perfection.

variants: Write the best thing about your trading this week.
Note one thing you're proud of from this week's trading.
Write one honest good thing about how you traded this week.
tasks.why: Recognizing progress keeps you motivated.

variants: Count how many of the past 7 days you followed your setup rules.
Check your journal — how many on-plan days did you have this week?
Tally up your yes/no check-ins for the past 7 days.
tasks.why: Weekly discipline score shows you your real consistency.

variants: Write your net dollar result for the past week.
Add up wins and losses for the week.
Write your total P&L for days 22-28.
tasks.why: Weekly results are more meaningful than daily ones.

variants: Write one thing you'll do differently next week.
Name one habit to work on this coming week.
Write your single focus for the next 7 days.
tasks.why: One goal per week leads to real change.

variants: Log today's trades: ticker, result, dollars.
Add today's trades to your journal.
Record today's session in your trade log.
tasks.why: Keep the logging habit strong heading into month 2.

variants: Write yes or no — did you follow your rules today?
Mark today as on-plan or off-plan.
Check your rules and rate today.
tasks.why: Daily check-ins are now your most important habit.

variants: Write what you'll focus on in month 2.
Write one goal for the next 30 days of trading.
Name the one thing you want to be better at by day 60.
tasks.why: Setting a focus heading into month 2 gives direction.

variants: Count your total wins and losses for days 15-30.
Look at your journal and tally up the past two weeks: wins vs losses.
Write your win/loss count for the second half of month 1.
tasks.why: Month 1 is almost done — see where you stand.

variants: Write your net dollar result for the past 30 days.
Add up wins and losses for the full month.
Calculate your total P&L for the first 30 days.
tasks.why: Month-end numbers tell you the honest story.

variants: Write two sentences about what you learned in month 1.
Summarize month 1 in 2 sentences.
Write what surprised you most about your trading in the first 30 days.
tasks.why: Reflection at month-end locks in what you learned.

variants: Log today's trades: ticker, result, dollars.
Add today's session to your journal.
Record today's trades before you close out.
tasks.why: Month 2 starts with the same daily habit.

variants: Write yes or no — did you follow your rules today?
Mark today on-plan or off-plan.
Rate your discipline today.
tasks.why: The daily check is still your most important habit.

variants: Write whether your focus for month 2 still feels right.
Check the goal you set for month 2 — is it still the right one?
Write one sentence about what you want to get better at this month.
tasks.why: Reviewing your goal at the start of a new month keeps it alive.

variants: Log today's trades: ticker, result, dollars.
Fill in your journal for today.
Add today's trades to your log.
tasks.why: Keep logging every day.

variants: Write yes or no — did you follow your rules today?
Mark today as on-plan or off-plan.
Rate today's rule-following.
tasks.why: The check-in habit is more valuable than any single trade.

variants: Write how many times you moved your stop loss after entering today.
Note if you moved your stop at all during today's trades.
Write yes or no — did you move your stop after entering a trade today?
tasks.why: Moving stops is a major discipline issue for experienced traders.

variants: Log today's trades: ticker, result, dollars.
Add today's trades to your journal.
Record today's session.
tasks.why: Logging is the backbone of improvement.

variants: Write yes or no — did you follow your rules today?
Mark today on-plan or off-plan.
Write your discipline rating for today.
tasks.why: Honest daily ratings add up to real insight.

variants: Write one note about your trade size today — too big, too small, or right?
Write whether your position size felt comfortable today.
Note if you traded too large or too small today.
tasks.why: Size affects how you feel and decide in a trade.

variants: Log today's trades: ticker, result, dollars.
Fill in your trade journal for today.
Add today's session to your log.
tasks.why: Every entry matters.

variants: Write yes or no — did you follow your rules today?
Mark today as on-plan or off-plan.
Check your rules and rate today.
tasks.why: The daily check takes seconds and pays dividends.

variants: Write whether you exited any trade too early today.
Note if you left money on the table by exiting before your target.
Write yes or no — did you cut a trade before your target today?
tasks.why: Cutting winners early is as costly as letting losers run.

variants: Log today's trades: ticker, result, dollars.
Add today's trades to your journal.
Record today before you shut down.
tasks.why: Five weeks in — the habit is solid if you've kept it up.

variants: Write yes or no — did you follow your rules today?
Mark today on-plan or off-plan.
Rate your rule-following today.
tasks.why: Keep the streak.

variants: Write the week's win count and loss count so far.
Tally up this week's wins and losses.
Write your win vs loss count for the past 5 trading days.
tasks.why: Mid-week check-ins help you catch bad patterns early.

variants: Log today's trades: ticker, result, dollars.
Fill in your journal for today.
Add today to your trade log.
tasks.why: Log it even if it was a quiet day.

variants: Write yes or no — did you follow your rules today?
Mark today as on-plan or off-plan.
Rate today's discipline.
tasks.why: The daily check never gets old.

variants: Write whether you traded when you didn't have a clear setup today.
Note if you entered any trades that didn't really fit your setup.
Write yes or no — did you trade a setup that wasn't really there today?
tasks.why: Forcing trades is one of the most expensive habits to break.

variants: Log today's trades: ticker, result, dollars.
Record today's session in your journal.
Add today's trades to your log.
tasks.why: Consistent logging makes weekly reviews easy.

variants: Write yes or no — did you follow your rules today?
Mark today on-plan or off-plan.
Rate your discipline today.
tasks.why: The check-in keeps you honest.

variants: Write the one rule you break most often.
Name the rule you find hardest to follow.
Write which of your rules you've broken most this week.
tasks.why: Naming the hardest rule is the first step to following it.

variants: Log today's trades: ticker, result, dollars.
Add today's trades to your journal.
Fill in today's trade log.
tasks.why: The habit matters more than any single day's results.

variants: Write yes or no — did you follow your rules today?
Mark today as on-plan or off-plan.
Write your rule score for today.
tasks.why: Keep the daily check going.

variants: Write one sentence about how you managed your emotions today.
Write whether you stayed calm or got emotional during trades today.
Note if fear or greed affected any of your decisions today.
tasks.why: Emotional awareness is a skill that improves with practice.

variants: Log today's trades: ticker, result, dollars.
Add today's session to your journal.
Record today's trades before you close out.
tasks.why: Keep logging.

variants: Write yes or no — did you follow your rules today?
Mark today on-plan or off-plan.
Rate your discipline today.
tasks.why: Daily accountability never gets easier to skip — that's the point.

variants: Write how many trades you took this week vs your planned limit.
Compare this week's trade count to what you planned.
Write if you over-traded or under-traded relative to your plan this week.
tasks.why: Comparing plan to reality shows where discipline breaks down.

variants: Count your on-plan days for the past week.
Tally your yes/no check-ins for the past 7 days.
Check your journal — how many of the last 7 days were on-plan?
tasks.why: Weekly discipline score is more telling than daily.

variants: Write your net dollar result for the past week.
Add up this week's wins and losses.
Calculate your weekly P&L.
tasks.why: Week 6 results show if your habits are paying off.

variants: Write one thing you'll improve next week.
Name the single habit to work on this coming week.
Write your one focus for the next 7 days.
tasks.why: One clear focus per week drives real change.

variants: Log today's trades: ticker, result, dollars.
Add today's session to your journal.
Record today's trades.
tasks.why: Log it and move on.

variants: Write yes or no — did you follow your rules today?
Mark today as on-plan or off-plan.
Rate today's rule-following.
tasks.why: The daily check is now second nature.

variants: Write whether you're trading the same setup you wrote on day 1 or something different.
Note if your go-to setup has changed since you started.
Write yes or no — are you still trading the same setup from day 1?
tasks.why: Checking your setup consistency reveals discipline or drift.

variants: Log today's trades: ticker, result, dollars.
Fill in your journal for today.
Add today's trades to your log.
tasks.why: Six weeks of logging is a real track record.

variants: Write yes or no — did you follow your rules today?
Mark today on-plan or off-plan.
Write your discipline score for today.
tasks.why: The habit is the most valuable thing you're building.

variants: Write one thing the journal has taught you that you didn't expect.
Write a surprise insight you've gotten from logging your trades.
Note something you've learned about yourself from your trade journal.
tasks.why: Unexpected insights are often the most valuable ones.

Add today's session to your trade log.
Record today's trades in your journal.
tasks.why: Keep the streak alive.

variants: Write yes or no — did you follow your rules today?
Mark today as on-plan or off-plan.
Check your rules and rate today.
tasks.why: Daily checks are the heartbeat of your trading discipline.

variants: Write what you could have done differently on your worst trade today.
Note one change that would have improved your biggest loss today.
Write one lesson from your most painful trade today.
tasks.why: One lesson per bad trade prevents repeating the same mistake.

variants: Log today's trades: ticker, result, dollars.
Add today's trades to your journal.
Fill in today's trade log.
tasks.why: Log every trading day — no exceptions.

variants: Write yes or no — did you follow your rules today?
Mark today on-plan or off-plan.
Rate your discipline today.
tasks.why: The check is fast and worth it.

variants: Write how many days you've logged in a row.
Count your current logging streak and write it.
Write your streak count — how many consecutive days have you logged?
tasks.why: Streaks are motivating and worth protecting.

variants: Count total wins vs losses for the past 30 days.
Go through your journal and tally wins and losses for days 15-45.
Write your win/loss count for your 30 days of daily logging.
tasks.why: Thirty days of data gives you something reliable to work with.

variants: Write your net dollar result for the past 30 days.
Add up all your wins and losses since you started daily logging.
Calculate your total P&L for days 15-45.
tasks.why: One month of daily data tells the real story.

variants: Write two sentences about how your trading has changed since month 1.
Summarize what's improved in your trading over the past month.
Write one thing you do better now than you did 30 days ago.
tasks.why: Progress worth noting keeps you going.

variants: Log today's trades: ticker, result, dollars.
Add today's session to your journal.
Record today's trades.
tasks.why: Phase 3 starts now — keep the daily log going.

variants: Write yes or no — did you follow your rules today?
Mark today on-plan or off-plan.
Rate your discipline today.
